text:
NUCLEAR MEDICINE / PET IMAGING REPORT

Examination: Ga-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT, Whole Body
Clinical Indication: Castration-resistant prostate cancer, Gleason 9 (Grade Group 5), germline BRCA2 mutation. Currently on ola 200 mg BID (dose-reduced from 300 mg BID due to grade 3 anemia). Prior treatment: ADT + Zytiga/prednisone (progressed), enza (progressed with new bone metastases, T10/T12 and right 4th rib). Palliative radiation delivered to T10-T12 vertebral metastases (20 Gy/5 fractions) prior to ola initiation. Assessment of treatment response on Lynparza at approximately 4 months. PSA at time of scan: 1.4 ng/mL (declined from 9.2 ng/mL pre-ola).

Technique: Following intravenous administration of approximately 185 MBq (5.0 mCi) of Ga-68 PSMA-11, whole-body PET acquisition was performed from skull base to mid-thigh, 60 minutes post-injection. Concurrent low-dose CT was performed for attenuation correction and anatomic correlation. Oral and intravenous contrast not administered for the CT component. Images were reconstructed using standard iterative algorithm. SUVmax values measured using lean body mass correction.

Comparison: Prior Tc-99m bone scan performed approximately 9 months prior to current study (at time of Xtandi progression, showing new lesions in T10, T12, right 4th rib, with mixed picture in previously known lumbar spine, pelvis, and right femur lesions).

---

FINDINGS:

HEAD AND NECK:
No PSMA-avid lesions identified in the brain parenchyma, calvarium, or cervical soft tissues. The major salivary glands (parotid and submandibular) demonstrate expected physiologic PSMA uptake bilaterally, which is not significantly elevated compared to background normal glandular activity. No pathologic cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy with PSMA avidity.

THORAX:
Lungs: The bilateral pulmonary nodules identified on prior CT imaging are not assessable by this scan given absence of diagnostic contrast CT; however, no focal PSMA-avid lesions are identified within the lung parenchyma at this time. No new pulmonary nodules with PSMA avidity. Mediastinum and hila: No PSMA-avid mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. No pleural effusions.

Thoracic Spine / Ribs:
T10 and T12 vertebral bodies: Post-radiation changes noted on CT correlation, with sclerotic remodeling of both vertebral bodies. PSMA uptake in T10 and T12 is markedly reduced compared to what would be expected given the avidity of disease at other sites prior to treatment; residual faint uptake present in T10 (SUVmax approximately 2.8), consistent with post-radiation changes and/or treated/suppressed viable tumor. CT demonstrates no epidural soft tissue component at T10 or T12 on the non-contrast images (prior MRI showed epidural extension at T10 with moderate canal compromise; post-radiation change noted). T12 demonstrates near-background PSMA activity (SUVmax 1.9).
Right 4th rib metastasis: Previously identified on bone scan as new at time of Xtandi progression. There is a small sclerotic lesion on CT at the posterior right 4th rib with only faint residual PSMA uptake (SUVmax 2.4), markedly decreased from expected pre-treatment avidity, suggesting good treatment response at this site.
No new osseous lesions identified in the thorax.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: No PSMA-avid hepatic lesions. Liver parenchyma is homogeneous on the CT component. No hepatomegaly. The absence of PSMA-avid hepatic disease is noted.
Spleen: Normal size and PSMA activity (physiologic).
Kidneys and Urinary Tract: Bilateral kidneys demonstrate expected physiologic high-level PSMA uptake. There is normal tracer excretion via the urinary collecting system. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses.
Adrenal Glands: No PSMA-avid adrenal lesions. Bilateral adrenal glands are unremarkable on CT.
Pancreas, Bowel, Stomach: No focal PSMA-avid abnormalities.

Lumbar Spine:
L2, L3, and L4 vertebral bodies: CT demonstrates sclerotic bony lesions consistent with known osseous metastases, previously documented on initial CT and bone scans. PSMA uptake at these sites is substantially reduced compared to what was described at time of enza progression. L2 (SUVmax 3.1), L3 (SUVmax 2.6), L4 (SUVmax 2.9). These values reflect markedly diminished activity relative to pre-treatment expectations for high-grade metastatic prostate cancer, and are interpreted as indicating significant suppression of metabolically active disease at these sites. No new lumbar vertebral lesions.

PELVIS:
Osseous pelvis: The previously described sclerotic/lytic mixed lesions in the sacrum, bilateral sacroiliac joints (right greater than left), and right iliac wing show similar findings. PSMA uptake at these sites is low-level: sacrum (SUVmax 2.7), right iliac wing (SUVmax 3.0). The bilateral sacroiliac joint lesions demonstrate residual but notably reduced PSMA avidity. No new pelvic osseous lesions.
Pelvic lymph nodes: The right obturator node, previously measuring 2.1 cm at diagnosis and 1.3 cm at 4 months on Xtandi (per prior CT), measures approximately 1.0 cm on the CT component of the current study. No definite PSMA avidity above background in the right obturator region. Left external iliac region: No PSMA-avid lymphadenopathy identified. No new pelvic lymphadenopathy with PSMA avidity.
Prostate bed: The patient has not had definitive local therapy to the prostate (no prostatectomy, no definitive radiation). The prostate gland region demonstrates only background PSMA activity; any residual primary site disease does not demonstrate significant PSMA avidity at this time.

RIGHT FEMUR:
Right femoral neck and proximal shaft: Previously documented osseous metastatic involvement. CT demonstrates sclerotic remodeling. PSMA uptake at the right femoral neck is reduced to near-background levels (SUVmax 2.3). No fracture. No new femoral lesions.

SOFT TISSUES:
No PSMA-avid soft tissue masses or lymph nodes identified in any imaged region outside of those described above.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Substantial reduction in PSMA avidity throughout the known osseous metastatic sites (lumbar spine L2-L4, pelvis including sacroiliac joints and iliac wing, right femur, right 4th rib) compared to the activity expected at time of Xtandi progression, consistent with a favorable metabolic response to Lynparza therapy. Residual low-level uptake at several sites likely reflects treated disease.

2. Post-radiation changes at T10 and T12 vertebral bodies with only faint residual PSMA activity at these sites; no epidural soft tissue component is evident on the CT component, suggesting interval improvement from prior MRI-documented epidural extension at T10.

3. No PSMA-avid visceral metastases identified -- no hepatic, pulmonary, or adrenal lesions with abnormal PSMA uptake.

4. No PSMA-avid pelvic or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy; the previously enlarged right obturator node appears smaller and without significant tracer uptake.

5. No new osseous, nodal, or visceral sites of PSMA-avid disease.

Overall, findings are consistent with meaningful disease suppression across osseous and nodal sites in the setting of ongoing ola therapy and PSA decline to 1.4 ng/mL.
